```mermaid
    lineFC --> lineTC
    lineTC --> pointBEP
    lineTR --> pointBEP
    pointBEP --> regionLoss
    pointBEP --> regionProfit
    pointBEP --> noteBEP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the chart:** The TR line starts at the origin (0,0) and slopes upward at angle $\tan^{-1}(P)$. The TC line starts at $(0, FC)$ on the Y-axis and slopes upward at angle $\tan^{-1}(V)$. Their intersection — the BEP — divides the plane into the **loss region** (left of BEP) and the **profit region** (right of BEP).

---

## 4.2 Cost Decomposition — Food Processing Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    totalCost["TOTAL COST TC"]
    fixedCost["FIXED COST FC"]
    varCost["VARIABLE COST VC"]
    fcItems["Depreciation, Salaries, Rent, Insurance, Interest"]
    vcItems["Raw Materials, Packaging, Direct Labor, Freight, Utility Energy"]
    semiVar["SEMI VARIABLE e.g. Electricity Bill"]
    semiSplit["Decompose into Fixed plus Variable parts"]
    totalCost --> fixedCost
    totalCost --> varCost
    totalCost --> semiVar
    semiVar --> semiSplit
    semiSplit --> fixedCost
    semiSplit --> varCost
    fixedCost --> fcItems
    varCost --> vcItems
```

---

## 4.3 Techno-Economic Feasibility Report (TEFR) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    tefr["TECHNO ECONOMIC FEASIBILITY REPORT for FOOD PROCESSING"]
    ts["TECHNICAL SECTION"]
    fs["FINANCIAL SECTION"]
    ms["MARKET SECTION"]
    ls["LEGAL AND MANAGERIAL SECTION"]
    tsA["Process Flow Diagram"]
    tsB["Machinery List and Capacity"]
    tsC["Raw Material Sourcing Plan"]
    tsD["Plant Layout and Utilities"]
    fsA["Capital Investment Estimate"]
    fsB["Working Capital Estimate"]
    fsC["BEP Calculation"]
    fsD["Payback, ROI, NPV, IRR"]
    msA["Demand Supply Gap"]
    msB["Competitor Analysis"]
    msC["Pricing Strategy"]
    lsA["FSSAI License"]
    lsB["Pollution NOC"]
    lsC["MSME Udyam Registration"]
    lsD["Manpower Plan"]
    tefr --> ts
    tefr --> fs
    tefr --> ms
    tefr --> ls
    ts --> tsA
    ts --> tsB
    ts --> tsC
    ts --> tsD
    fs --> fsA
    fs --> fsB
    fs --> fsC
    fs --> fsD
    ms --> msA
    ms --> msB
    ms --> msC
    ls --> lsA
    ls --> lsB
    ls --> lsC
    ls --> lsD
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Flow — From Opportunity Identification to TEFR Approval

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    opp["Identify Food Processing Opportunity"]
    scout["Idea Scouting and Market Survey"]
    pre["Pre Feasibility Screening"]
    tefrDraft["Draft TEFR with BEP and Payback"]
    review["Review by Banker and Industry Expert"]
    go["GO Decision - Setup Plant"]
    nogo["NO GO Decision - Reject or Pivot"]
    opp --> scout
    scout --> pre
    pre --> tefrDraft
    tefrDraft --> review
    review --> go
    review --> nogo
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define Break-Even Point (BEP). Explain the contribution margin concept with a food industry example.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Break-Even Point (BEP)** is the volume of sales at which **Total Revenue (TR) exactly equals Total Cost (TC)**, resulting in **zero profit and zero loss**.
>
> **Contribution Margin** = Selling Price − Variable Cost per unit. It is the amount each unit contributes toward recovering fixed costs and thereafter generating profit.
>
> **Food industry example:** In a bakery producing bread loaves at ₹40 each, if variable cost per loaf is ₹25 (flour, yeast, packaging, direct labor), the contribution per loaf is ₹15. If monthly fixed cost is ₹75,000, then BEP = 75,000 ÷ 15 = **5,000 loaves/month**.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**List any six mandatory components of a Techno-Economic Feasibility Report (TEFR) for a food processing unit.**

**Model Answer:**

1. Executive Summary
2. Project Profile & Product Description
3. Market Analysis (demand-supply, competition)
4. Technical Feasibility (process, machinery, capacity)
5. Financial Feasibility (capital cost, working capital, BEP, payback, ROI)
6. Statutory & Legal Compliance (FSSAI, Pollution NOC, GST)

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Three common mark-loss triggers in Module-4 answers:
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in BEP** (writing "30,000" instead of "30,000 kg"). Always append the unit (kg, L, packets).
> 2. **Confusing BEP in units vs. BEP in rupees.** Examiners allocate separate sub-marks. State both clearly with labels.
> 3. **Omitting the MoS interpretation.** Don't just calculate; explain "this means the unit can absorb a 62.5 % drop in sales before turning loss-making."

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A food technologist plans to set up a **small-scale ready-to-drink (RTD) mango beverage unit** with the following data:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Installed annual capacity | 1,50,000 bottles (200 mL each) |
| Selling price | ₹25 per bottle |
| Variable cost per bottle | ₹16 (mango pulp + sugar + water + bottle + cap + label + direct labor) |
| Annual fixed cost | ₹5,40,000 |

Calculate:
1. BEP in **bottles** and in **₹**.
2. **Profit** at 1,20,000 bottles of annual sales.
3. **Margin of Safety** in units and as a percentage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **structure of a TEFR** for the above RTD mango beverage unit. List the technical, financial, and statutory elements you would include.

#### Model Solution

**Given:**
- $P = ₹25$/bottle
- $V = ₹16$/bottle
- $FC = ₹5,40,000$/year
- $C = P - V = ₹9$/bottle

**1(a) BEP in bottles:**

$$\text{BEP}_Q = \frac{FC}{P - V} = \frac{5,40,000}{9}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BEP}_Q = 60,000 \text{ bottles per year}}$$

**1(b) BEP in ₹:**

$$\text{BEP}_{\text{Rs}} = \text{BEP}_Q \times P = 60,000 \times 25$$

$$\boxed{\text{BEP}_{\text{Rs}} = ₹15,00,000 \text{ per year}}$$

**2. Profit at 1,20,000 bottles:**

$$\text{Profit} = (P - V) \cdot Q - FC = 9 \times 1,20,000 - 5,40,000$$

$$= 10,80,000 - 5,40,000$$

$$\boxed{\text{Profit} = ₹5,40,000 \text{ per year}}$$

**3. Margin of Safety:**

$$\text{MoS}_{\text{units}} = 1,20,000 - 60,000 = 60,000 \text{ bottles}$$

$$\text{MoS Ratio} = \frac{60,000}{1,20,000} \times 100 = 50\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{MoS} = 60,000 \text{ bottles } (50\%)}$$

**Interpretation:** The unit enjoys a comfortable 50 % cushion — sales can drop by half before losses occur.

**(b) Structure of TEFR for the RTD Mango Beverage Unit:**

| Section | Components |
|---|---|
| **Executive Summary** | One-paragraph snapshot: product, capacity, investment, BEP, payback. |
| **Product Profile** | 200 mL tetra-packed mango RTS beverage; FSSAI category 14.1; shelf life 6 months. |
| **Market Analysis** | Demand for RTD fruit beverages in Kerala; CAGR of the segment; competitor scan (Paper Boat, Real, Tropicana); pricing strategy. |
| **Technical Plan** | Process flow: Mango selection → washing → pulping → pasteurization → blending → homogenization → UHT or hot-fill → packaging → QC → storage. Machinery list — pulper, pasteurizer (₹ X), homogenizer (₹ Y), Aseptic filler (₹ Z), CIP system. Plant capacity 1,50,000 bottles/year on 1-shift. |
| **Locational Plan** | Site near mango-growing district (e.g., Palakkad) for raw material access; proximity to NH for distribution. |
| **Financial Plan** | Capital investment — Land & building ₹30 lakh, Machinery ₹45 lakh, Working capital ₹15 lakh. **BEP = 60,000 bottles; MoS = 50 %; Payback ≈ 3.5 years.** |
| **Statutory Compliance** | FSSAI license (₹7,500 for turnover > ₹20 lakh), Kerala State Pollution Control Board consent, MSME/Udyam registration, GST registration, Factory license (if > 10 workers with power). |
| **Risk & SWOT** | Mango price volatility, seasonal supply, competition from large brands, equipment breakdown. |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **Margin of Safety** and **P/V Ratio**. A small-scale **tomato ketchup unit** has the following annual data:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Selling price | ₹90 per kg |
| Variable cost | ₹55 per kg |
| Annual fixed cost | ₹7,00,000 |
| Actual annual sales | 40,000 kg |

Calculate:
1. BEP in kg and ₹.
2. Margin of Safety (kg and %).
3. P/V Ratio.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **limitations of Break-Even Analysis** as applied to a food processing unit. How can multi-product BEP be handled in a unit producing **tomato ketchup** and **tomato sauce**?

#### Model Solution

**(a) Margin of Safety and P/V Ratio — Definition:**

- **Margin of Safety (MoS)** = Excess of actual (or budgeted) sales over break-even sales. It indicates the **cushion** before losses start.
- **P/V Ratio** = Contribution ÷ Sales × 100 = $(P-V)/P \times 100$. It measures the **rate at which profit changes with sales**.

**Numerical Solution:**

Given: $P = ₹90$, $V = ₹55$, $FC = ₹7,00,000$, $Q_{act} = 40,000$ kg

**1. BEP:**

$$\text{BEP}_Q = \frac{7,00,000}{90 - 55} = \frac{7,00,000}{35} = 20,000 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{BEP}_{\text{Rs}} = 20,000 \times 90 = ₹18,00,000$$

**2. Margin of Safety:**

$$\text{MoS}_{\text{units}} = 40,000 - 20,000 = 20,000 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{MoS Ratio} = \frac{20,000}{40,000} \times 100 = 50\%$$

**3. P/V Ratio:**

$$\text{P/V Ratio} = \frac{90 - 55}{90} \times 100 = \frac{35}{90} \times 100 \approx 38.89\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{P/V Ratio} \approx 38.89\%}$$

**(b) Limitations of BEA & Multi-Product Handling:**

**Limitations (any 4 valid points):**
1. **Linearity assumption** — Variable cost per unit is not always constant (bulk discounts, seasonal raw material price swings).
2. **Constant sales-mix assumption** — Tomato ketchup and tomato sauce have different contribution margins and the mix varies with season.
3. **Fixed cost strictly fixed only in relevant range** — Beyond a threshold, additional shifts, supervisory staff, etc., may be required.
4. **Ignores time value of money** — BEP is a static single-period snapshot.
5. **Capital structure ignored** — Interest may move between fixed and operational depending on debt-equity mix.
6. **External shocks ignored** — FSSAI recalls, pest outbreaks (e.g., tomato leaf curl virus), supply-chain disruptions.

**Multi-Product BEP Handling:**

For a unit selling tomato ketchup and tomato sauce, the **weighted-average P/V ratio** method is used.

**Step 1:** Determine sales revenue proportion $w_i$ of each product.

**Step 2:** Compute individual P/V ratios: $(P_i - V_i)/P_i$.

**Step 3:** Weighted P/V = $\sum w_i \cdot (\text{P/V}_i)$.

**Step 4:**
$$\text{Combined BEP}_{\text{Rs}} = \frac{FC_{\text{total}}}{\text{Weighted P/V Ratio}}$$

The combined BEP in rupees is then apportioned to each product based on the actual sales mix. This approach is more realistic because it acknowledges that ketchup and sauce carry different margins.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for Module 4 (BEA + TEFR)**

- **Core formula** — $\text{BEP}_Q = \dfrac{FC}{P - V}$ — must be memorized with units.
- **BEP in ₹** = $\dfrac{FC}{\text{P/V Ratio}}$ or BEP(units) × P.
- **Contribution per unit** = P − V. **P/V Ratio** = C / P × 100.
- **Margin of Safety** = Actual Sales − BEP Sales. Healthy MoS ≥ 25 %.
- **MoS Ratio > 50 %** = excellent cushion (rare in newly commissioned food units).
- **Multi-product BEP** uses **weighted-average P/V ratio** based on sales-mix.
- **TEFR** must contain **9 sections** — Executive Summary, Product, Market, Technical, Locational, Financial, Managerial, Statutory, Risk.
- **FSSAI license** is mandatory for every food unit — costing ₹100 (basic) to ₹7,500 (state).
- **Healthy food industry BEP** ≤ 60 % of installed capacity.
- **Payback period** for food processing = 3–5 years (typical).
- **Limitations of BEA** — linearity, constant mix, ignores TVM, ignores capital structure.
- Always **label units** — kg, L, packets, bottles — to score full marks.
- **Cross-verify** by computing BEP in ₹ two ways — (FC/PV ratio) and (BEP units × P).
- **Show all derivation steps** when solving KTU problems — partial marks depend on it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bakery and Confectionary, Fruit and Vegetable Processing, Oil and Fat, Milk and Milk Products, etc.
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT837 - ENTREPRENEURSHIP DEVELOPMENT IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bakery and Confectionary, Fruit and Vegetable Processing, Oil and Fat, Milk and Milk Products, etc.

# Module 4 — Food Processing Industry Sub-Sectors: Entrepreneurial Perspectives

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Food Processing Sub-Sector Entrepreneurship"?

From the KTU 2024 Scheme OEFTT837 perspective, this module is **not** about becoming a food chemist. It is about recognizing the **business opportunity** in converting raw agricultural commodities (wheat, fruits, vegetables, oilseeds, milk) into **shelf-stable, value-added, branded products** that can be sold to a defined consumer market.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Food processing sub-sector entrepreneurship* refers to the identification, planning, establishment, and operation of small-to-medium scale industrial units engaged in the **transformation of primary food materials into secondary/tertiary processed products** within the bakery-confectionery, horticulture (F&V), oilseeds & fats, and dairy verticals, with the objective of generating **value addition, employment, and economic returns** under Indian regulatory frameworks (FSSAI, MSME, APEDA, MPEDA, BIS).

### 1.2 The Four Sub-Sectors — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The "Kitchen-to-Factory" Ladder):**
> Think of a small entrepreneur as climbing a ladder from the home kitchen (raw commodity) to the industrial factory (branded product). Each rung adds **shelf-life, marketability, and price margin**. Module 4 walks you through four of the most accessible rungs for a Kerala-based first-generation entrepreneur.

| Sub-Sector | Raw Material (Rung 1) | Value-Added Product (Rung 4) | Typical Unit Investment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bakery & Confectionery** | Wheat flour, sugar, fats | Bread, biscuits, cakes, toffees, chocolates | ₹5–50 lakh |
| **Fruit & Vegetable Processing** | Fresh fruits & vegetables | Jam, squash, pickles, dehydrated products, pulp | ₹10–80 lakh |
| **Oil & Fat Processing** | Oilseeds (coconut, groundnut, mustard) | Refined oil, vanaspati, ghee, margarine | ₹15–1 crore |
| **Milk & Milk Products** | Raw milk | Ghee, paneer, curd, flavored milk, ice cream | ₹5–60 lakh |

### 1.3 The Common Entrepreneurial Logic Across All Four

Every sub-sector follows the same **"5M Framework"** of entrepreneurship:

1. **Market** — Who will buy? At what price? Through which channel?
2. **Man** — Skilled labor, food technologist, quality manager.
3. **Material** — Continuous supply of raw material at uniform quality.
4. **Money** — Capital investment, working capital, break-even horizon.
5. **Machinery** — Processing equipment sized to the targeted capacity.

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU exam line: *"The success of a food processing enterprise depends less on the technology and more on the **assured supply chain and brand penetration**."*

## 2. Sector-Wise Product Profile and Market Snapshot

### 2.1 Bakery & Confectionery Sector

- **Bread** (white, brown, whole wheat, multigrain) — daily staple, mass market.
- **Biscuits & Cookies** — high shelf-life, huge rural demand.

- **Confectionery** — toffees, éclairs, hard-boiled candy, chewing gum, mints, chocolates.

- **Puff pastries, khari, rusk** — tea-time accompaniments.

**Indian Market Size (approx.):** ₹60,000+ crore organized bakery market; **biscuit** segment alone is ~₹35,000 crore (Britannia, Parle, ITC dominant). Kerala has a strong local market — *Priya, Karikkkin, Adithi, Elite* are household names.

### 2.2 Fruit & Vegetable (F&V) Processing

- **Preserves** — Jam, jelly, marmalade, preserve.
- **Beverages** — RTS beverages, fruit juice, nectar, squash, crush, cordial.
- **Pickles & Chutneys** — oil-based, vinegar-based, salted.

> **Kerala's USP:** Banana, coconut, jackfruit, mango, pineapple, cashew apple, passion fruit, and spices. The state has a strong **"Kerala Banana Chips"** GI-tagged product.

### 2.3 Oil & Fat Processing

- **Primary processing** — Oil extraction (expeller, solvent).
- **Secondary processing** — Refining (degumming, neutralization, bleaching, deodorization — **RBD**).

  - **Ghee** from milk fat / vegetable ghee.

  - **Specialty fats** — cocoa butter equivalents, ice-cream fats.

> [!NOTE]
> **Entrepreneurial hotspot in Kerala:** Virgin Coconut Oil (VCO) and cold-pressed coconut oil — high export demand, premium pricing (₹400–600/litre).

## 3. Why These Four Sectors for an Entrepreneur?

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hook:** A 14-mark question in Module 4 often asks: *"Justify the selection of the bakery/confectionery/fruit-vegetable/oil/dairy sector for an entrepreneurial venture in Kerala."*

**Justification Pillars:**

1. **Raw material abundance** — Kerala produces coconut, banana, jackfruit, milk, and cashew in surplus.
2. **Low-to-moderate capital entry barrier** — bakery and pickle units can start at ₹3–10 lakh.
3. **Year-round demand** — bakery and dairy are daily-need; F&V products have festival seasonality.
4. **FSSAI registration simplicity** — Basic Registration for turnover < ₹12 lakh; State License for ₹12 lakh–₹20 crore; Central License > ₹20 crore.
5. **Government subsidies** — PM Kisan Sampada Yojana, KAU-Kerala Food Commission subsidies, NABARD, KSIDC, KFCB.
6. **Employment generation** — High ratio of unskilled + semi-skilled labor absorption.
7. **Export potential** — VCO, banana chips, spices, cashew, pickles to Gulf and Europe.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 Bakery & Confectionery — Process Logic & Technology

### 2.1.1 The Bread-Making Process (Straight-Dough Method)

The scientific backbone is the **gluten–water–yeast triad** plus controlled thermal treatment.

$$\text{Flour} + \text{Water} + \text{Yeast} + \text{Salt} \xrightarrow{\text{Mix}} \text{Dough} \xrightarrow{\text{Ferment}} \text{Sponge} \xrightarrow{\text{Shape}} \text{Panned} \xrightarrow{\text{Proof}} \text{Baked} \to \text{Bread}$$

**Why each step is needed:**

- **Mixing:** Hydrates gluten, develops viscoelastic network, distributes yeast.
- **Fermentation (proofing):** Yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) converts glucose to CO₂ + ethanol:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Yeast}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 \uparrow$$

The CO₂ gets trapped in the gluten mesh → dough **rises**. The ethanol vaporizes during baking → contributes to **bread aroma**.

- **Baking (220–240 °C):** Starch gelatinizes (~60–70 °C), gluten coagulates (~75 °C), Maillard browning → crust forms.

### 2.1.2 Confectionery Sugar Stages — The "Sugar Curve" (High-Yield Topic)

Sugar syrups are heated to specific temperatures and cooled to specific concentrations to yield different confectionery textures. This is a **favourite KTU question**.

| Stage | Temperature (°C) | Water Content | Sugar Conc. | Product |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Thread stage** | 107–110 | 30% | 80% | Glacé, syrups |
| **Soft ball** | 112–115 | 14% | 86% | Fudge, fondant |
| **Firm ball** | 118–120 | 10% | 90% | Caramels |
| **Hard ball** | 121–130 | 6% | 94% | Toffees, divinity |
| **Soft crack** | 132–143 | 4% | 96% | Butterscotch |
| **Hard crack** | 146–154 | 2% | 98% | Lollipops, hard candy |
| **Caramel** | 160–170 | 1% | 99% | Caramel color, candy coating |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU exams:** When asked "which sugar stage is used for toffee manufacturing?" — answer **"Hard ball (121–130 °C)"**. For jam — answer **"Final concentration 68 °Brix"**.

### 2.1.3 Jam Manufacturing — The Gel Theory

Pectin (a polysaccharide) + Sugar (≥ 60–65%) + Acid (pH 3.0–3.4) → three-dimensional gel network.

$$\text{Pectin} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Sugar} \xrightarrow{\text{pH}<3.5} \text{Three-dimensional gel (Jam)}$$

**High-methoxyl pectin (HMP)** requires ≥ 60% soluble solids and pH 2.8–3.5. **Low-methoxyl pectin (LMP)** requires calcium ions and does not need as much sugar.

### 2.1.4 Biscuit Manufacturing — The Creaming Process

- **Ingredients:** Refined wheat flour, sugar, shortening/fat, leavening agent, milk solids, salt, flavor.
- **Creaming** = Sugar + Fat blended to incorporate air → tender texture.
- **Types of biscuits:** Hard dough (Nimki, Marie), short dough (Glucose, Bourbon), rotary moulded (Milk Bikis), wire-cut, sandwich, cream-cracker.

### 2.1.5 Chocolate Manufacturing (Crystallization-Dependent)

- Cocoa mass + cocoa butter + sugar + milk solids → **conching** (refining aroma) → **tempering** (controlled crystallization of cocoa butter into Form V crystals) → moulding.
- **Tempering temperatures (dark chocolate):** cool to 27–28 °C, reheat to 31–32 °C.

### 2.1.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Bakery/Confectionery

| Formula / Metric | Expression / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Baker's percentage | $\text{Ingredient \%} = \dfrac{W_{\text{ingredient}}}{W_{\text{flour}}} \times 100$ | % |
| Fermentation time | 1.5–3 h at 28–32 °C | h |
| Oven temperature (bread) | 220–240 | °C |
| Baking time (bread) | 25–35 | min |
| Total soluble solids (jam) | 68 ± 1 | °Brix |
| Sugar-acid-pectin ratio (jam) | 60 : 1 : 1 (TSS : pectin : acid) | ratio |
| Tempering (dark chocolate) | 31–32 | °C |
| Biscuit moisture | < 4 | % |
| Bread moisture | 35–40 | % |
| Toffee sugar concentration | 94 (hard ball) | % |

---

## 2.2 Fruit & Vegetable Processing — Theory

### 2.2.1 The "Why Process?" Logic

> [!NOTE]
> *F&V are highly perishable* — Kerala loses 30–40% of horticultural produce post-harvest. Processing:
> 1. Extends shelf-life from days → months/years.
> 2. Adds value 3× to 10×.
> 3. Generates off-season revenue.
> 4. Opens export markets.

### 2.2.2 The Four Pillars of F&V Preservation

1. **Aseptic / Thermal processing** — pasteurization, sterilization, canning.
2. **Reduction of water activity (a_w)** — dehydration, sugar addition, salt addition.
3. **Reduction of pH** — fermentation, acidification.
4. **Use of chemical preservatives** — KMS (potassium metabisulphite), sodium benzoate, sorbic acid.

### 2.2.3 Water Activity (a_w) — The Master Equation

$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0} = \frac{ERH}{100}$$

Where $p$ = partial vapor pressure of water in food; $p_0$ = vapor pressure of pure water; $ERH$ = equilibrium relative humidity (%).

- Most bacteria: require $a_w > 0.91$
- Most yeasts: $a_w > 0.87$
- Most moulds: $a_w > 0.80$
- **Microbial growth generally halts below $a_w = 0.60$**

### 2.2.4 Pickle Fermentation

Anaerobic lactic acid fermentation by *Lactobacillus* species in salt brine:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{LAB}} 2\,\text{Lactic Acid} + 2\,H^+ + 2\,ATP$$

Salt concentration 8–15% initially → inhibits spoilage → allows LAB to dominate.

### 2.2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — F&V Processing

| Parameter | Expression / Value |
|---|---|
| TSS (jam) | 68 °Brix |
| TSS (squash) | 40–50 °Brix |
| TSS (RTS beverage) | 10–15 °Brix |
| KMS dosage | 350–700 ppm |
| Sodium benzoate | 750–1000 ppm |
| Pickling salt | 8–15% w/v |
| Drying temperature (cabinet) | 55–70 °C |
| Fruit: sugar ratio (squash) | 1 : 1.5 to 1 : 2 |
| Vinegar pH (pickle) | < 4.0 |
| Blanching temp (vegetable) | 90–95 °C, 3–5 min |

---

## 2.3 Oil & Fat Processing — Theory

### 2.3.1 Oil Extraction Logic

$$\text{Oilseed} \xrightarrow{\text{Cleaning}} \xrightarrow{\text{Dehulling}} \xrightarrow{\text{Condtioning}} \xrightarrow{\text{Expeller (mechanical)}} \xrightarrow{\text{Solvent (hexane)}} \text{Crude Oil + Meal}$$

- **Expeller (mechanical pressing):** Recovery ~ 65–75%; cold-pressed possible.
- **Solvent extraction:** Recovery > 99%; but downstream refining needed.

### 2.3.2 Refining Sequence (RBDO)

$$\text{Crude Oil} \to \text{Degumming} \to \text{Neutralization} \to \text{Bleaching} \to \text{Deodorization} \to \text{RBD Oil}$$

| Step | Removes | Reagent |
|---|---|---|
| **Degumming** | Phospholipids, gums | Water, phosphoric acid |
| **Neutralization** | Free fatty acids (FFA) | NaOH (caustic soda) |
| **Bleaching** | Pigments (carotene, chlorophyll) | Activated earth, carbon |
| **Deodorization** | Volatile odoriferous compounds | Steam at 220–260 °C, vacuum |

**Saponification value (SV):** measure of molecular weight of fat.

$$SV = \dfrac{56.1 \times V \times N}{W}$$

Where $V$ = mL KOH, $N$ = normality, $W$ = weight of oil (g), 56.1 = MW of KOH.

**Iodine value (IV):** measure of unsaturation.

**Acid value (AV):** mg KOH needed to neutralize FFA in 1 g oil.

### 2.3.3 Hydrogenation (Vanaspati)

$$\text{Unsaturated oil} + H_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Ni catalyst, 160–200 °C}} \text{Semi-solid fat (Vanaspati)}$$

- Produces semi-solid fat, used as cheap ghee substitute.
- Generates **trans fats** — modern regulations discourage (FSSAI cap: industrial trans fat < 2%).

### 2.3.4 Virgin Coconut Oil (VCO) — Kerala Entrepreneurial Star

- **Cold process:** Fresh coconut milk → fermentation → separation → filtration → VCO.
- **Hot process:** Dried coconut meat (copra) → pressing → VCO.
- **Centrifuge method:** Coconut milk centrifuged → cream → VCO.
- No chemical refining → retains tocopherols, polyphenols.
- Premium price: ₹400–800/litre in domestic & export market.

### 2.3.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Oils & Fats

| Parameter | Expression / Typical |
|---|---|
| Refining loss | 5–8% of crude oil weight |
| Acid value (refined) | < 0.5 mg KOH/g |
| Iodine value (coconut) | 7–12 |
| Iodine value (sunflower) | 110–140 |
| Iodine value (groundnut) | 85–100 |
| Saponification value | 190–210 (most edible oils) |
| VCO moisture | < 0.1% |
| VCO FFA (as lauric) | < 0.5% |
| Trans fat cap (FSSAI) | < 2% by weight |
| Hydrogenation temperature | 160–200 °C |

## 2.4 Milk & Milk Products — Theory

### 2.4.1 Composition of Milk (Cow vs Buffalo vs Human)

| Component | Cow | Buffalo | Human |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fat %** | 3.5–4.5 | 6.0–7.5 | 3.5–4.0 |
| **Protein %** | 3.2–3.5 | 4.0–4.5 | 1.0–1.5 |
| **Lactose %** | 4.5–5.0 | 4.5–5.0 | 6.5–7.5 |
| **Ash (minerals) %** | 0.7–0.8 | 0.7–0.8 | 0.2–0.3 |
| **Water %** | 86–88 | 82–84 | 87–88 |
| **SNF (Solids-Not-Fat) %** | 8.5–9.0 | 9.0–10.0 | 8.5–9.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FSSAI Standard (Cow Milk):** Fat ≥ 3.5%, SNF ≥ 8.5%. **Buffalo Milk:** Fat ≥ 5.0%, SNF ≥ 9.0%. **Mixed Milk:** Fat ≥ 4.5%, SNF ≥ 8.7%.

### 2.4.2 Pasteurization — The Time-Temperature Equation

$$F_{0} = 10^{\dfrac{T - 121.1}{z}}$$

Where $z$ = 10 °C for most vegetative pathogens.

**Common Pasteurization (HTST):** 72 °C × 15 s.
**UHT:** 135–150 °C × 2–4 s.
**Boiling (kitchen):** 100 °C × 5 min.

### 2.4.3 Paneer Making

$$\text{Milk} \xrightarrow{\text{Heat 90 °C)}} \xrightarrow{\text{Citric acid / Lactic acid}} \text{Curd} \xrightarrow{\text{Filtration}} \text{Paneer}$$

- Yield: ~ 18–20% (cow) and ~ 22–25% (buffalo).
- Paneer is acid-coagulated; **cheese** is enzyme (rennet/microbial) coagulated.

### 2.4.4 Ghee — The Clarified Butter

- Butter → heated to 110–120 °C → moisture evaporated → milk solids browned → filtered.
- NF-AGMARK grades: Special, General.
- Vitamin A retention: 80–90%.

### 2.4.5 Fermented Milk (Curd/Yogurt)

$$C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} + H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Lactase}} 2\,C_6H_{12}O_6$$
$$\text{Glucose} \xrightarrow{\text{Lactobacillus}} 2\,\text{Lactic Acid}$$

Lactic acid → pH drop to 4.0–4.5 → casein coagulation → set curd.

### 2.4.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Dairy

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Pasteurization (HTST) | 72 °C × 15 s |
| Pasteurization (LTLT) | 63 °C × 30 min |
| UHT | 135 °C × 2–4 s |
| Standardized milk (FSSAI) | 4.5% fat, 8.5% SNF |
| Toned milk | 3.0% fat, 8.5% SNF |
| Double-toned milk | 1.5% fat, 9.0% SNF |
| Curd pH | 4.0–4.5 |
| Paneer yield (cow) | 18–20% |
| Paneer yield (buffalo) | 22–25% |
| Ghee yield from cream | 80–82% |
| Ghee moisture | < 0.5% |
| Ice cream overrun | 70–100% |

$$\text{Overrun (\%)} = \dfrac{W_{\text{mix}} - W_{\text{frozen}}}{W_{\text{mix}}} \times 100$$

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Frameworks & Derivations

This section provides **exhaustive, KTU-examination-style derivations** of critical entrepreneurial decision formulas. Every transition is explained in words.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Break-Even Point (BEP) for a Bakery Unit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU syllabus hook:** "Apply break-even analysis to evaluate the feasibility of a small-scale bread/biscuit/confectionery unit."

### Step 1: Define the parameters

Let $S$ = total sales revenue (₹/year), $C_f$ = fixed cost (₹/year), $C_v$ = variable cost (₹/year), $Q$ = units sold per year, $p$ = selling price per unit, $c$ = variable cost per unit.

$$S = p \times Q \quad \text{(Total Sales)}$$

$$C_v = c \times Q \quad \text{(Total Variable Cost)}$$

### Step 2: Set up the profit equation

Profit $\pi$ is:

$$\pi = S - C_f - C_v = p\,Q - C_f - c\,Q$$

$$\pi = (p - c)\,Q - C_f$$

### Step 3: Define Break-Even Point (BEP)

At BEP, profit $\pi = 0$:

$$0 = (p - c)\,Q_{BE} - C_f$$

### Step 4: Solve for $Q_{BE}$ (units)

$$(p - c)\,Q_{BE} = C_f$$

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{C_f}{p - c}$$

The denominator $(p - c)$ is called the **contribution margin per unit** ($CM$):

$$CM = p - c$$

### Step 5: Solve for Break-Even Sales (₹)

$$S_{BE} = p \times Q_{BE} = p \times \frac{C_f}{p - c}$$

Or equivalently:

$$S_{BE} = \frac{C_f}{1 - \dfrac{c}{p}}$$

The denominator is the **P/V ratio (Profit-Volume ratio)**:

$$P/V = 1 - \frac{c}{p} = \frac{p - c}{p}$$

### Step 6: Numerical Example (Worked Out)

**Scenario:** A biscuit unit.
- Fixed cost = ₹ 3,00,000/year (rent, supervisor salary, depreciation).
- Selling price per kg biscuit = ₹ 200.
- Variable cost per kg = ₹ 140 (raw material, packing, fuel, commission).
- Working days = 300 days/year.

**Step 6a:** Compute contribution margin.

$$CM = 200 - 140 = \text{₹ } 60 \text{ per kg}$$

**Step 6b:** Compute BEP units.

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{300000}{60} = 5000 \text{ kg/year}$$

**Step 6c:** Compute BEP in days.

$$Q_{BE,\text{per day}} = \frac{5000}{300} = 16.67 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 6d:** Compute P/V ratio.

$$P/V = \frac{60}{200} = 0.30 \text{ or } 30\%$$

**Step 6e:** Compute BEP sales.

$$S_{BE} = \frac{300000}{0.30} = \text{₹ } 10,00,000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 6f:** Margin of Safety (MoS) at sales ₹15 lakh.

$$MoS = \frac{S - S_{BE}}{S} = \frac{1500000 - 1000000}{1500000} = 33.33\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's cue:** A higher MoS % means the enterprise is **safer** from sales drop. KTU students often confuse BEP with MoS — remember, BEP is the *floor*, MoS is the *cushion above the floor*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Jam Recipe Calculation Using Sugar-Acid-Pectin Rule

### Step 1: Set the standard

**Standard jam composition:**
- Total soluble solids (TSS) = **68 °Brix**
- Sugar : Pectin : Acid = **60 : 1 : 1** (rule of thumb)

### Step 2: Define inputs

- Pulp weight: $W_p$ (kg)
- Sugar to be added: $W_s$ (kg)
- Pectin to be added: $W_{pec}$ (kg)
- Acid (citric) to be added: $W_{cit}$ (kg)
- Water to be added: $W_w$ (kg)
- Total batch weight: $W_T = W_p + W_s + W_{pec} + W_{cit} + W_w$

### Step 3: Apply the sugar-percentage constraint

Sugar in final mix should be 60% of the total weight (more specifically, 60–65% of total solids):

$$W_s = 0.60 \times W_T$$

But practical recipe solves as: sugar = 60% of the **total weight of fruit pulp + sugar** (excluding water) plus pectin-acid adjustment.

**Numerical Example:**

Take 100 kg mango pulp, TSS in pulp = 14 °Brix (i.e., 14 kg solids, 86 kg water in pulp).

**Target:** final TSS = 68 °Brix in jam.

**Step 3a:** Total soluble solids needed in final jam from pulp:

$$S_{pulp} = 100 \times 0.14 = 14 \text{ kg solids}$$

**Step 3b:** Let added sugar = $W_s$ kg. Added sugar = 100% solids.

**Step 3c:** Pectin and citric are negligible in weight, water is mostly lost in boiling. Assume final jam weight after boiling = $W_f$ kg.

$$W_f \times 0.68 = S_{pulp} + W_s$$

$$W_f \times 0.68 = 14 + W_s$$

**Step 3d:** If we target final jam weight = 100 kg (eaten-able yield), then:

$$100 \times 0.68 = 14 + W_s$$

$$68 = 14 + W_s$$

$$W_s = 54 \text{ kg sugar to add}$$

**Step 3e:** Pectin (if pulp is poor) = 1% of fruit weight = **1 kg**.
**Step 3f:** Citric acid = 0.5–1% of fruit weight = **0.5–1 kg**.
**Step 3g:** Water = small amount (5–10% of pulp) to dissolve sugar.

**Step 3h:** Validate total cook-down. Pulp (100) + Sugar (54) + Pectin (1) + Acid (0.7) + Water (7) = **162.7 kg initial mix**. Boil to ~100 kg final jam (62% weight loss = water evaporation). ✔

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Average weight loss during jam cooking is 30–40% (mostly water). Match this against the theoretical yield.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Acid Value & FFA Calculation in Oil

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner cue:** Acid value is the most frequently asked parameter in oil quality questions.

### Definition
**Acid Value (AV):** Milligrams of KOH required to neutralize the free fatty acids in **1 gram** of oil.

$$AV = \frac{56.1 \times V_{KOH} \times N_{KOH}}{W_{sample}}$$

Where:
- $56.1$ = equivalent weight of KOH (g/mol)
- $V_{KOH}$ = volume of KOH solution used (mL)
- $N_{KOH}$ = normality of KOH solution (N)
- $W_{sample}$ = weight of oil taken (g)

### Relation to Free Fatty Acid % (as oleic)

$$FFA \,\% \, (\text{as oleic}) = \frac{AV \times 0.503}{1}$$

The 0.503 is the **conversion factor** (molecular weight of oleic acid = 282.5; 1 mL 1N KOH = 0.0282 g oleic; per gram oil: 0.0282/0.0561 = 0.503 — wait, let's derive cleanly).

**Clean derivation:**

1 mL of 1N KOH neutralizes $\frac{56.1}{1000} = 0.0561$ g KOH = 0.0561 × 1 = 0.0561 gram-equivalents.

Moles of KOH in $V$ mL of $N$ normality = $\dfrac{V \times N}{1000}$ mol.

This same number of moles will neutralize an equal number of moles of FFA (1:1 stoichiometry).

Mass of oleic acid neutralized = $\dfrac{V \times N}{1000} \times 282.5$ g.

Per gram of oil:

$$FFA \% = \frac{V \times N \times 282.5 \times 100}{1000 \times W} = \frac{V \times N \times 28.25}{W}$$

Divide $AV$ by 2 to obtain % oleic equivalently (since $AV$ is defined for KOH neutralization per gram oil):

$$FFA \, \% \, \text{(as oleic)} = \frac{AV}{2}$$

Wait — **clarification of the industry standard:**

For oils, the common conversion is:

$$FFA \, \% \, \text{(as oleic acid)} = \frac{AV \times 0.503}{1}$$

This factor **0.503** comes from $\frac{\text{M.W. of KOH}}{\text{M.W. of oleic}} = \frac{56.1}{282.46} = 0.1986$ — half of that is 0.0993, so the *correct* factor is:

$$FFA \, \% = \frac{AV \times 0.503}{1} \text{ (often written as AV/2)}$$

The factor **0.503** is widely accepted; the formula is:

$$FFA \, \% \, \text{(as oleic)} = \frac{AV}{1.99} \approx \frac{AV}{2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU exams, write:** "$FFA\% = AV/2$ (as oleic)" — this is the most defensible form.

### Numerical Example

- Oil sample: 5 g
- KOH used in titration: 2.5 mL
- KOH normality: 0.1 N

**Step 1:** Calculate Acid Value.

$$AV = \frac{56.1 \times 2.5 \times 0.1}{5} = \frac{14.025}{5} = 2.805 \, \text{mg KOH/g}$$

**Step 2:** Convert to FFA %.

$$FFA \% = \frac{2.805}{2} = 1.40\% \, \text{(as oleic)}$$

**Step 3:** Quality verdict (refined oil should have AV < 0.5). This oil is **crude** (high FFA) — needs refining.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Standardization of Milk (Fat & SNF Adjustment)

**Problem:** You have 1000 L cow milk with 3.0% fat and 8.0% SNF. You need to make it **standardized milk** with 4.5% fat and 8.5% SNF.

### Step 1: Principle

- **To increase fat:** Add **cream** (high fat) OR remove skim milk.
- **To increase SNF (or both fat and SNF):** Add **powdered skim milk (SMP)** OR remove water.
- **To decrease fat:** Add **skim milk** (or remove cream).

Here we need to *raise* both fat and SNF → add cream **and** SMP.

### Step 2: Pearson's Square Method (Single Component)

Suppose we have 8% SNF skim milk and 36% SNF SMP, and we want 8.5% SNF standardized milk.

Pearson's Square:

$$
\begin{array}{lcc}
\text{SMP (36\%)} & & \text{Difference (8.5 - 8.0 = 0.5)} \\
& 8.5 & \\
\text{Skim Milk (8.0\%)} & & \text{Difference (36 - 8.5 = 27.5)} \\
\end{array}
$$

Ratio of SMP : Skim Milk = **0.5 : 27.5 = 1 : 55**.

**Meaning:** To make 100 kg standardized milk, mix 1.79 kg SMP + 98.21 kg skim milk. Verify:

$$\text{SNF in mix} = \frac{(1.79 \times 0.36) + (98.21 \times 0.08)}{100} = 0.643 + 7.857 = 8.50\% \checkmark$$

### Step 3: Fat Standardization (Two-Component)

Suppose cream is 40% fat and skim milk is 0.05% fat; we want 4.5% fat standardized milk from 3.0% fat raw milk.

**Option A:** Add cream. But cream also adds SNF — adjust separately.

In practice, dairies use **cream (40% fat) and SMP (36% protein) and water** to engineer the final 4.5% F / 8.5% SNF milk.

### Step 4: Mass balance (linear system)

Let $x$ = kg cream added, $y$ = kg SMP added, $z$ = kg water added to 1000 L (≈ 1030 kg) of raw milk (3.0% fat, 8.0% SNF).

**Fat balance:**

$$1030 \times 0.030 + x \times 0.40 = (1030 + x + y + z) \times 0.045$$

**SNF balance:**

$$1030 \times 0.080 + y \times 0.36 = (1030 + x + y + z) \times 0.085$$

**Volume consistency:** $z = 0$ (if we only add dry ingredients).

**Step 5: Solve simultaneously (let $W = 1030 + x + y$):**

Fat:

$$30.9 + 0.40\,x = 0.045\,W$$

SNF:

$$82.4 + 0.36\,y = 0.085\,W$$

Subtracting $\dfrac{0.045}{0.085} = 0.529$ times the SNF equation from the fat equation:

$$30.9 + 0.40\,x - 0.529\,(82.4 + 0.36\,y) = 0$$

$$30.9 + 0.40\,x - 43.6 - 0.190\,y = 0$$

$$0.40\,x - 0.190\,y = 12.7$$

From mass balance: $W = 1030 + x + y$.

Substitute $W$ into fat equation:

$$30.9 + 0.40\,x = 0.045 \times (1030 + x + y)$$

$$30.9 + 0.40\,x = 46.35 + 0.045\,x + 0.045\,y$$

$$0.355\,x - 0.045\,y = 15.45 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

From (i) and second equation — solve. (Actual numerical solution omitted; KTU students are expected to set up the system, not necessarily solve the multi-variable case unless specifically asked.)

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU exams**, the typical question is a **two-component Pearson's Square** — not full mass balance. Always be ready to draw the square.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Yield of Paneer from Milk

### Setup
- Cow milk: 4.0% fat, 8.5% SNF → total solids = 12.5%.
- Buffalo milk: 6.5% fat, 9.5% SNF → total solids = 16.0%.

### Theoretical yield (assuming all solids retained):

$$Y_{theor} = \text{Total Solids \%} \times 0.85 \text{ (recovery factor)}$$

- Cow paneer yield: $12.5\% \times 0.85 = 10.6\% \rightarrow$ but **practical yield is 18–20%** because of moisture retention and added solids.
- Buffalo paneer yield: $16.0\% \times 0.85 = 13.6\% \rightarrow$ **practical 22–25%**.

### Industry benchmark formula:

$$Y_{paneer} = 1.8 \times \text{Fat \%} - 1.5 \text{ (for cow milk, approx)}$$

For cow milk with 4% fat:

$$Y_{paneer} = 1.8 \times 4 - 1.5 = 7.2 - 1.5 = 5.7\%$$

This underestimates because moisture in paneer is high (~55–60%). Realistic mass-balance-based yield:

$$Y_{paneer} = \frac{\text{Casein \%} \times 100}{\text{100 - moisture\% of paneer}}$$

Paneer moisture ≈ 55–60% (regulated by FSSAI: < 70% for fresh paneer). Casein in cow milk ≈ 2.6%.

$$Y_{paneer} = \frac{2.6}{100 - 60} \times 100 = 6.5\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **For exams, give an empirical rule:** *1 L cow milk ≈ 180–200 g paneer; 1 L buffalo milk ≈ 220–250 g paneer.*

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Ghee Yield from Butter / Cream

### Method (Direct from Butter)
- Take 100 kg butter (~80% fat, 16% water, 4% SNF).
- Heat to 110–120 °C → water evaporates → milk solids brown → filter.

**Step 1:** Theoretical yield = fat content = 80 kg.

**Step 2:** Practical yield (allow 2% loss as browned residue) = 78 kg.

### Method (From Cream)
- Cream 40% fat, 60% serum.
- Churn to butter → heat butter to ghee.
- **Yield:** ~ 80% of butter weight in ghee.

### Formula

$$Y_{ghee} = \text{Fat \% of source} \times \frac{1}{0.99} - L$$

Where $L$ = loss factor (1–2%).

**Numerical:** From 1000 L milk (4% fat, 3.6% fat recovery as cream → 100 L cream @ 40% fat).

Cream to butter: ~ 50% → 50 kg butter.
Butter to ghee: 80% → **40 kg ghee from 1000 L milk**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard rule:** 25–28 L cow milk → 1 kg ghee; 15–18 L buffalo milk → 1 kg ghee.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*List the FSSAI classification of milk and state the legal standards of fat and SNF for cow and buffalo milk.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) classifies market milk based on fat content. The legal standards are:

| Type of Milk | Minimum Fat % | Minimum SNF % |
|---|---|---|
| **Toned Milk** | 3.0 | 8.5 |
| **Double Toned Milk** | 1.5 | 9.0 |
| **Standardized Milk** | 4.5 | 8.5 |
| **Full Cream Milk** | 6.0 | 9.0 |
| **Cow Milk (raw)** | 3.5 | 8.5 |
| **Buffalo Milk (raw)** | 5.0 | 9.0 |
| **Mixed Milk (raw)** | 4.5 | 8.7 |

(3 marks for full table with SNF column)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*What is the significance of the "hard ball stage" in confectionery? Mention the temperature and water content.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The **hard ball stage** is a critical sugar-cooking stage in confectionery corresponding to a syrup temperature of **121–130 °C** and a water content of about **6%** (sugar concentration ~ 94%).
- At this stage, when a small quantity of syrup is dropped into cold water, it forms a **firm, hard ball** that holds its shape but is still slightly plastic.
- It is the **standard stage for manufacturing toffees, caramels, and divinity fudge**, where a chewy, non-sticky, firm body is desired.
- If cooked further, the syrup reaches the **hard crack stage (146–154 °C)**, used for hard candies and lollipops.
- Going beyond this leads to **caramelization** (160–170 °C), used for color and flavor development in bakery and confection.

(2 marks hard ball definition; 1 mark for applications)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B gives a **Module-level choice**. Below are two alternative questions, each worth 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** *Discuss the manufacturing process of bread using the straight-dough method. Add a note on the role of each ingredient.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *An entrepreneur in Kochi plans to set up a small bakery producing 200 kg of biscuits per day. The fixed cost is ₹ 4,00,000 per year and the variable cost is ₹ 150 per kg. The selling price is ₹ 220 per kg. Calculate the break-even point in units, in rupees, and in number of working days (300 days per year). What is the margin of safety if actual sales are 80,000 kg per year?* **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

The **straight-dough method** is a single-stage bread-making process where all ingredients are added at once, mixed, fermented, and baked.

**1. Ingredients and their roles (3.5 marks):**

| Ingredient | Role |
|---|---|
| **Refined wheat flour (maida)** | Provides gluten (glutenin + gliadin) for viscoelastic dough network. |
| **Water** | Hydrates gluten (≥ 60% hydration), enables starch gelatinization. |
| **Yeast (*S. cerevisiae*)** | Ferments sugar → CO₂ + ethanol; CO₂ causes dough to rise. |
| **Sugar** | Substrate for yeast + contributes to browning (Maillard) and crust color. |
| **Salt** | Tightens gluten, regulates yeast, enhances flavor. |
| **Shortening/fat** | Tenderizes crumb, increases shelf-life, lubricates dough. |
| **Milk solids** | Improves crust browning, flavor, nutritional value. |
| **Improver (ascorbic acid)** | Strengthens gluten, improves dough handling. |

**2. Process steps (3.5 marks):**

1. **Weighing and sifting** of dry ingredients.
2. **Mixing** — all ingredients in spiral mixer for 15–20 min → developed dough.
3. **Bulk fermentation** — 1.5–3 h at 28–32 °C, 75% RH → dough rises.
4. **Punching and scaling** — degassing + dividing into loaf-sized pieces.
5. **Rounding, sheeting, moulding, panning.**
6. **Final proofing** — 45–60 min at 35–40 °C, 85% RH.
7. **Baking** — 220–240 °C for 25–35 min → starch gelatinizes, gluten coagulates, Maillard crust forms.
8. **Depanning, cooling, slicing, packing.**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Given:**
- Production = 200 kg/day
- Fixed cost ($C_f$) = ₹ 4,00,000/year
- Variable cost ($c$) = ₹ 150/kg
- Selling price ($p$) = ₹ 220/kg
- Working days = 300/year
- Actual sales = 80,000 kg/year

**Step 1: Contribution margin per unit (1 mark)**

$$CM = p - c = 220 - 150 = \text{₹ } 70/\text{kg}$$

**Step 2: Break-Even Point in units (1.5 marks)**

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{C_f}{CM} = \frac{400000}{70} = 5714.3 \text{ kg/year}$$

**Step 3: Break-Even Point in rupees (1.5 marks)**

$$S_{BE} = p \times Q_{BE} = 220 \times 5714.3 = \text{₹ } 12,57,143 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 4: Break-Even Point in working days (1 mark)**

$$Q_{BE,\text{per day}} = \frac{5714.3}{300} = 19.05 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 5: Margin of Safety (MoS) at actual 80,000 kg (2 marks)**

$$MoS = \frac{S - S_{BE}}{S} \times 100$$

First compute $S_{actual}$:

$$S_{actual} = 220 \times 80000 = \text{₹ } 1,76,00,000$$

$$MoS = \frac{17600000 - 1257143}{17600000} \times 100 = 92.86\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q.A):**
> 1. **Skipping units in BEP** — students often write "5714" without specifying kg/year. Always write **"kg/year"** or **"kg/day"**.
> 2. **Confusing BEP sales with total sales** — $S_{BE}$ is the revenue at which profit = 0, not the total revenue earned.
> 3. **MoS formula sign error** — always use $S - S_{BE}$ in the numerator; never $S_{BE} - S$ (negative result is meaningless in this context).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** *Explain the manufacturing of jam. Discuss the role of pectin, sugar, and acid in gel formation. State the standard for TSS in jam.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A sample of crude coconut oil weighs 5 g. In titration, 3.6 mL of 0.1 N KOH is used. Calculate the Acid Value and the % FFA (as oleic). Comment on the suitability of the oil for direct refining.* **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Jam is a fruit preserve** made by boiling fruit pulp with sugar to a concentration of **68 °Brix**, with pectin and acid facilitating gel formation.

**1. Manufacturing process (3 marks):**

1. **Selection of fruit** — ripe, sound, free from decay (mango, pineapple, mixed fruit, jackfruit).
2. **Washing, peeling, deseeding, slicing.**
3. **Pulping** — fruit is passed through pulper or pulper-finisher.
4. **Boiling** — pulp + sugar (in 1:1 to 1:1.25 ratio) + pectin (1%) + citric acid (0.5–1%) + KMS (350 ppm) is boiled in stainless steel pan with steam coil.
5. **End point** — TSS = 68 °Brix (verified by refractometer), or "sheet test" passes.
6. **Filling hot** — into pre-sterilized glass jars at ≥ 85 °C.
7. **Sealing, cooling, labeling.**

**2. Role of each component in gel formation (3 marks):**

- **Pectin** — a polysaccharide (galacturonic acid polymer). In the **HMP (High-Methoxyl Pectin)** form, it forms a **3-D network** when the conditions are right. Pectin molecules aggregate via hydrogen bonds.
- **Sugar** — at ≥ 60%, sugar **competes with pectin for water**, dehydrating the pectin network and stabilizing the gel.
- **Acid** — reduces pH to 2.8–3.5, **neutralizing the negative charges** on pectin carboxyl groups → reduces electrostatic repulsion → pectin chains can approach and bond.

**The classical rule for jam gel:**

$$\text{TSS} \ge 68\% \quad \text{AND} \quad pH \le 3.5 \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Pectin} \ge 1\%$$

If any one of these is missing, the gel will not set.

**3. Standard for TSS in jam (1 mark):**

- FSSAI standard: **TSS not less than 68 %** (refractometric, corrected to 20 °C).
- Minimum fruit content: 45% for most jams (except specific fruits).

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Given:**
- $W_{sample}$ = 5 g
- $V_{KOH}$ = 3.6 mL
- $N_{KOH}$ = 0.1 N

**Step 1: Acid Value (3.5 marks)**

$$AV = \frac{56.1 \times V \times N}{W} = \frac{56.1 \times 3.6 \times 0.1}{5}$$

$$AV = \frac{20.196}{5} = 4.039 \approx 4.04 \text{ mg KOH/g}$$

**Step 2: % FFA as oleic (2.5 marks)**

$$FFA \% = \frac{AV}{2} = \frac{4.04}{2} = 2.02\%$$

**Step 3: Suitability comment (1 mark):**

- A high FFA of **2.02%** indicates the oil is **crude/unrefined**.
- Refined coconut oil should have **FFA < 0.1% (as lauric)** or **AV < 0.5 mg KOH/g**.
- The oil **must undergo refining** (neutralization with NaOH to remove FFA) before bleaching and deodorization.
- The oil is **NOT directly suitable** for edible use in its current crude form.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q.B):**
> 1. **Forgetting 56.1 in acid value formula** — leads to 1/56.1 error in answer. Always show $\frac{56.1 \times V \times N}{W}$.
> 2. **Using wrong divisor for FFA%** — dividing AV by 1 instead of 2 (or multiplying by 0.503) is the commonest error.
> 3. **Not commenting on quality** — students often stop at the calculation. KTU award 1–2 marks for a **practical conclusion** (refined vs crude, suitable for direct consumption or not).

**1. Bakery & Confectionery**
- Bread: flour–water–yeast–salt. Fermentation = CO₂ + ethanol. Baking 220–240 °C.
- Sugar stages: thread (107 °C) → soft ball (112) → firm ball (118) → **hard ball (121–130) = toffee** → soft crack (132) → hard crack (146) → caramel (160).
- Jam: TSS = **68 °Brix**; pectin 1%, acid 0.5–1%, KMS 350 ppm.
- Biscuit moisture < 4%, bread moisture 35–40%.
- Chocolate: tempering at 31–32 °C (dark).

**2. Fruit & Vegetable Processing**
- Water activity: $a_w < 0.60$ → safe from microbes.
- Jam 68 °Brix; squash 40–50 °Brix; RTS 10–15 °Brix.
- KMS 350–700 ppm; sodium benzoate 750–1000 ppm; pickle salt 8–15%.
- Kerala: banana, jack, mango, pineapple, kokum, passion fruit.

**3. Oil & Fat Processing**
- Refining sequence: **D-N-B-D** (Degumming, Neutralization, Bleaching, Deodorization) = **RBD**.
- Acid Value = $\frac{56.1 \times V \times N}{W}$; FFA% = AV/2.
- Iodine value: coconut 7–12, sunflower 110–140, groundnut 85–100.
- VCO: premium, no chemical refining, ₹ 400–800/litre.
- Trans fat cap (FSSAI) = **< 2%**.

**4. Milk & Milk Products**
- Cow milk: 3.5–4.5% F / 8.5% SNF. Buffalo: 6–7.5% F / 9–10% SNF.
- HTST pasteurization: **72 °C × 15 s**. LTLT: 63 °C × 30 min. UHT: 135 °C × 2–4 s.
- Paneer: yield 18–20% (cow), 22–25% (buffalo).
- Ghee: 25–28 L cow milk → 1 kg ghee; 15–18 L buffalo milk → 1 kg ghee.
- Ice cream overrun = 70–100%.

**5. Cross-Cutting Entrepreneurial Concepts**
- FSSAI tier: Basic (< ₹ 12 L) / State (₹ 12 L – ₹ 20 Cr) / Central (> ₹ 20 Cr).
- BEP units = $C_f / (p - c)$. MoS % = $(S - S_{BE})/S \times 100$.
- P/V ratio = $(p - c)/p = CM/Sales$.
- MSME 2020: Micro ≤ 1 cr invest, Small ≤ 10 cr, Medium ≤ 50 cr.
- CFTRI Mysore = technology transfer partner; APEDA = export; AGMARK = voluntary quality mark; BIS = standard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Module 4 Answers:**
> 1. Open with the **definition** (1–2 lines).
> 2. Draw a **flow chart** (1 mark).
> 3. Justify **why this sector for Kerala** (2 marks).
> 4. Solve the **numerical** with units and labels (3–4 marks).
> 5. Close with **regulatory and FSSAI** compliance (1–2 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marketing Challenges and Approaches for New Products and Services Sustaining in Competitive Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT837 - ENTREPRENEURSHIP DEVELOPMENT IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Marketing Challenges and Approaches for New Products and Services Sustaining in Competitive Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marketing Challenges for New Products and Services** refer to the strategic, operational, regulatory, and consumer-acceptance barriers that an entrepreneur must overcome while introducing an innovative food product or service into a market that is already populated by established players.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sustaining in a Competitive Environment** is defined as the entrepreneur's ability to create, defend, and grow a defensible market position through continuous value creation, differentiation, and adaptive marketing strategies, such that the firm achieves long-term profitability, customer loyalty, and brand equity despite the entry of substitutes, new competitors, and shifting consumer preferences.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme for *OEFTT837 – Entrepreneurship Development in Food Industry*, this topic is mapped to **Course Outcome CO4** (Develop sustainable marketing and growth strategies for food enterprises) and is assessed under **Cognitive Levels L3 (Apply) and L4 (Analyze)** of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Restaurant Stall Analogy**
>
> Imagine a small entrepreneur setting up a traditional Kerala banana-chip stall in a town where three established players already sell similar snacks. The new entrant must overcome:
> - **Taste challenge** (Will customers prefer *my* flavor profile?),
> - **Visibility challenge** (How do customers even know I exist?),
> - **Trust challenge** (Why should they trust a new brand over a familiar one?),
> - **Price challenge** (Can I match or undercut the giants?),
> - **Sustainability challenge** (How do I keep my customers coming back after the first trial?).
>
> Marketing challenges are the *obstacles in the first month*. Sustainability in a competitive environment is the *ability to remain profitable in the fifth year*. The entrepreneur needs a **launch strategy** (a Marketing Mix) and a **retention strategy** (continuous innovation + brand equity) to survive.

### 1.3 Standard Marketing Metrics Used in This Module

The following metrics are universally applied in food-industry marketing analysis:

- **Market Share (MS):** The percentage of total category sales captured by a single firm. Expressed in **% (percentage)**.
- **Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR):** The mean year-over-year growth of a market or product line. Expressed in **% per annum**.
- **Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC):** The total marketing spend divided by the number of new customers. Expressed in **₹ per customer (INR)**.
- **Customer Retention Rate (CRR):** The percentage of existing customers retained over a given period. Expressed in **%**.
- **Net Promoter Score (NPS):** A loyalty metric ranging from **-100 to +100**.
- **Break-Even Volume:** The number of units that must be sold to cover all fixed and variable costs. Expressed in **units** or **kilograms**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students are expected to identify challenges, classify them, propose approaches (4Ps / 7Ps), and articulate sustainability frameworks such as Porter's Generic Strategies, the BCG Matrix, and the Product Life Cycle (PLC) as applied to food products.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Marketing Challenges for New Food Products

The challenges can be classified into **four functional domains** as follows:

**A. Consumer-Side Challenges (Demand-Side Friction)**
- **Trial Resistance:** Established food habits (e.g., Kerala consumers loyal to traditional *parippu vada*).
- **Taste Aversion Risk:** Food products trigger immediate sensory rejection if the first impression fails.
- **Health & Trust Perception:** Modern consumers scrutinize ingredients, FSSAI labelling, and additives.
- **Price Sensitivity:** Food is a high-frequency, low-involvement category where even a 5% price premium must be justified by visible value.

**B. Firm-Side / Operational Challenges (Supply-Side Friction)**
- **Cold-Chain Limitations:** Perishability of dairy, seafood, fresh produce.
- **Scale-Up Risk:** Pilot success may not translate to industrial-scale consistency.
- **Working Capital Crunch:** Inventory holding costs are extreme in food (FIFO + expiry).
- **Distribution Reach:** Inability to access modern trade (supermarkets) without listing fees.

**C. Regulatory & Environmental Challenges**
- **FSSAI 2006 Compliance:** Licensing, labelling standards, shelf-life declaration.
- **AGMARK / ISI / Organic Certification:** Costly for micro-entrepreneurs.
- **Food Safety Modernization:** Traceability, recall readiness.
- **Sustainability Pressure:** Demand for biodegradable packaging, reduced carbon footprint.

**D. Competitive Challenges**
- **Established Brand Equity:** Big players (Britannia, Nestle, ITC) have entrenched trust.
- **Private Label Threat:** Retailers (Reliance Fresh, BigBasket) launching own-brand products.
- **Imitation Speed:** Easy replication of successful food concepts.
- **Switching Cost for Consumer:** Essentially zero in food — switching is one-click.

### 2.2 Marketing Approaches (KTU High-Yield Strategies)

| # | Strategy | Application to Food Industry | Primary Lever |
|---|----------|------------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Mass Marketing (Undifferentiated)** | Staple foods (rice, sugar, salt) | Price + Distribution |
| 2 | **Segmented Marketing** | Vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free variants | Demographic / Psychographic |
| 3 | **Niche / Concentrated Marketing** | Organic baby food, keto snacks | Specialised channel |
| 4 | **Differentiated Marketing** | Multiple SKUs for different cities | Full 4Ps per segment |
| 5 | **Micro-Marketing** | Region-specific products (Malabar biryani masala) | Localised 4Ps |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are examinable in Part B (14-mark) numerical/analytical questions.

$$
\text{Market Share (MS)} = \frac{\text{Company Sales}}{\text{Total Category Sales}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{CAGR} = \left( \frac{\text{Ending Value}}{\text{Beginning Value}} \right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1
$$

$$
\text{Break-Even Volume (units)} = \frac{\text{Fixed Cost (₹)}}{\text{Unit Price (₹)} - \text{Variable Cost per Unit (₹)}}
$$

$$
\text{CAC} = \frac{\text{Total Marketing Spend (₹)}}{\text{Number of New Customers Acquired}}
$$

$$
\text{CRR (\%)} = \frac{\text{Customers at End} - \text{New Customers Acquired}}{\text{Customers at Start}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{NPS} = \%\,\text{Promoters} - \%\,\text{Detractors}
$$

$$
\text{Gross Margin (\%)} = \frac{\text{Selling Price} - \text{Cost of Goods Sold}}{\text{Selling Price}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Return on Marketing Investment (ROMI)} = \frac{\text{Incremental Revenue} - \text{Marketing Cost}}{\text{Marketing Cost}} \times 100
$$

| **Symbol / Term** | **Meaning** | **Standard Unit** |
|-------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| $n$ | Number of years / periods | years |
| $P$ | Unit Selling Price | ₹ / unit |
| $V$ | Variable Cost per Unit | ₹ / unit |
| $F$ | Total Fixed Cost | ₹ |
| $Q$ | Sales Quantity | units or kg |
| $\vert \Delta R \vert$ | Incremental Revenue (magnitude) | ₹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In the food industry, the entrepreneur uses these formulas to (a) decide market entry, (b) negotiate with retailers, (c) bid for institutional supply contracts, and (d) justify marketing budgets to banks and investors.

### 2.4 Sustainability Frameworks (The "How" of Long-Term Survival)

**A. Porter's Generic Strategies (Adapted to Food)**

1. **Cost Leadership:** Mass-produced staples (e.g., Aashirvaad atta).
2. **Differentiation:** Premium or artisanal positioning (e.g., Naga chilli oils).
3. **Focus (Niche):** Specialty ethnic, health, or religious products.

**B. The Product Life Cycle (PLC) Strategy Map**

| PLC Stage | Sales Trend | Marketing Objective | Promotional Lever |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Introduction | Slow rise | Trial + Awareness | Sampling, influencer push |
| Growth | Rapid climb | Differentiation | Advertising, distribution |
| Maturity | Plateau | Defend share | Loyalty programs, line extensions |
| Decline | Falling | Harvest / Divest | Discounting, niche retention |

**C. Ansoff Matrix for Growth Decisions**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
 & \text{Existing Products} & \text{New Products} \\
\text{Existing Markets} & \text{Market Penetration} & \text{Product Development} \\
\text{New Markets} & \text{Market Development} & \text{Diversification}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> For a *sustaining* strategy, food entrepreneurs most commonly rely on **Market Penetration** (loyalty, repeat purchase) and **Product Development** (new flavours, line extensions) — both lower-risk than diversification.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Break-Even Point for a New Food Product

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU 14-mark style):**
> An entrepreneur plans to launch a *traditional Kerala banana chips* brand in Thiruvananthapuram. The fixed cost of setting up the frying unit, branding, and FSSAI licensing is **₹ 5,00,000**. The variable cost per kg (raw banana, oil, salt, packaging) is **₹ 120**. The retail selling price is **₹ 220 per kg**. Determine (i) break-even volume, (ii) break-even revenue, and (iii) profit at 5,000 kg of monthly sales.

**Step 1 — Identify given values.**

$$
F = 5{,}00{,}000 \quad (\text{Fixed Cost in ₹}), \quad V = 120 \quad (\text{₹/kg}), \quad P = 220 \quad (\text{₹/kg})
$$

**Step 2 — Compute contribution margin per unit.**

$$
\text{Contribution Margin} = P - V = 220 - 120 = 100 \,\text{₹/kg}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the amount each kilogram sold contributes toward recovering the fixed cost.

**Step 3 — Apply the break-even formula.**

$$
Q_{BE} = \frac{F}{P - V} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{100}
$$

$$
Q_{BE} = 5{,}000 \,\text{kg}
$$

*Explanation:* The entrepreneur must sell exactly 5,000 kg to recover the entire fixed cost.

**Step 4 — Compute break-even revenue.**

$$
R_{BE} = P \times Q_{BE} = 220 \times 5{,}000
$$

$$
R_{BE} = 11{,}00{,}000 \,\text{₹}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute profit at Q = 5,000 kg.**

$$
\text{Profit} = (P - V) \times Q - F
$$

$$
\text{Profit} = 100 \times 5{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 0 \,\text{₹}
$$

*Explanation:* At 5,000 kg the firm breaks even — verifying Step 3.

**Step 6 — Compute profit at Q = 6,000 kg (realistic 20% growth).**

$$
\text{Profit} = 100 \times 6{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000 \,\text{₹}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpret for sustainability decision.**

A break-even of 5,000 kg in a single market signals that the entrepreneur must either *reduce fixed cost*, *raise price*, or *expand distribution* to multiple districts to sustain profitability.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Market Share and CAGR

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** A food entrepreneur's pickle brand had annual revenues of **₹ 40 lakh** in Year 1, **₹ 55 lakh** in Year 2, and **₹ 70 lakh** in Year 3. The total Indian pickle market is **₹ 5,000 crore** in Year 3. Calculate (i) market share in Year 3, (ii) 2-year CAGR.

**Step 1 — Convert to common units.**

$$
\text{Year 3 Revenue} = 70 \,\text{lakh} = 0.70 \,\text{crore}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute market share.**

$$
\text{MS} = \frac{0.70}{5{,}000} \times 100 = 0.014\,\%
$$

*Explanation:* The brand is a micro-player, occupying ~14 thousandths of a percent. Survival requires either a niche defence or aggressive growth.

**Step 3 — Compute CAGR over n = 2 years.**

$$
\text{CAGR} = \left( \frac{\text{End}}{\text{Start}} \right)^{1/n} - 1
$$

$$
\text{CAGR} = \left( \frac{55}{40} \right)^{1/2} - 1
$$

$$
\text{CAGR} = (1.375)^{0.5} - 1 = 1.1726 - 1 = 0.1726
$$

$$
\text{CAGR} \approx 17.26\,\% \,\text{per annum}
$$

*Explanation:* The brand is growing at 17.26% per year — healthy, but must be sustained for 5+ years to become a credible competitor.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Competitive Positioning (BCG)

| | High Market Share | Low Market Share |
|---|---|---|
| **High Growth** | ⭐ **Star** (Invest heavily) | ❓ **Question Mark** (Divest or invest) |
| **Low Growth** | 🐄 **Cash Cow** (Harvest cash) | 🐕 **Dog** (Liquidate) |

**Application to Food Industry:**

- **Star:** Plant-based meat alternatives in Indian metros.
- **Question Mark:** Cold-pressed oils in tier-3 towns (uncertain future).
- **Cash Cow:** Established brands like *Maggi* in India.
- **Dog:** Outdated beverage formulations in saturated markets.

A *sustaining* food entrepreneur converts **Question Marks → Stars** by investing in marketing, and defends **Stars → Cash Cows** by continuous product line extension.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — New Product Marketing Funnel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Idea Generation] --> B[Concept Testing]
    B --> C[Business Analysis]
    C --> D[Prototype Development]
    D --> E[Test Marketing]
    E --> F{Market Ready?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Full Launch]
    F -- No --> H[Reformulate]
    H --> D
    G --> I[Awareness Campaigns]
    I --> J[Trial via Sampling]
    J --> K[Repeat Purchase]
    K --> L[Brand Loyalty]
    L --> M[Sustained Market Position]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Marketing Mix (4Ps) for New Food Product

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Product
        P1[Quality Consistency]
        P2[Shelf Life]
        P3[Packaging Design]
        P4[Unique Selling Proposition]
    end
    subgraph Price
        PR1[Penetration Pricing]
        PR2[Premium Positioning]
        PR3[Bundle Pricing]
    end
    subgraph Place
        PL1[Modern Trade]
        PL2[Traditional Retail]
        PL3[D2C E Commerce]
        PL4[HoReCa Channel]
    end
    subgraph Promotion
        PRM1[Digital Ads]
        PRM2[Influencer Tie Ups]
        PRM3[Sampling Drives]
        PRM4[In Store Visibility]
    end
    Product --> Price
    Price --> Place
    Place --> Promotion
    Promotion --> Consumer
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Sustainability Loop in Competitive Environment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Competitor Gaps] --> B[Design Differentiated Offer]
    B --> C[Launch via 4Ps]
    C --> D[Capture Initial Trial]
    D --> E[Collect Feedback]
    E --> F{Profitable and Growing?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Reinforce Brand Equity]
    F -- No --> H[Pivot or Reformulate]
    H --> B
    G --> I[Line Extension]
    I --> J[New Market Entry]
    J --> A
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Porter's Five Forces Applied to Indian Food Industry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Center
        Rivalry[Rivalry Among Existing Competitors - HIGH]
    end
    Rivalry --> NewEntry[Threat of New Entrants - MEDIUM]
    Rivalry --> SubThreat[Threat of Substitutes - HIGH]
    Rivalry --> BuyerPower[Buyer Bargaining Power - MEDIUM]
    Rivalry --> SupplierPower[Supplier Bargaining Power - MEDIUM]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Product Life Cycle Marketing Strategy Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Introduction] -->|Slow Growth| B[Growth]
    B -->|Rapid Sales| C[Maturity]
    C -->|Plateau| D[Decline]
    A -.->|Sampling, Trial| A1[Objective Awareness]
    B -.->|Brand Building| B1[Objective Differentiation]
    C -.->|Loyalty| C1[Objective Defence]
    D -.->|Harvest| D1[Objective Cost Recovery]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*List any three major marketing challenges faced by an entrepreneur launching a new food product in Kerala.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**

1. **Consumer Trial Resistance:** Kerala consumers have strong food loyalties (e.g., traditional snacks), making trial of unfamiliar brands difficult.
2. **Distribution & Cold-Chain Limitations:** Reaching tier-2 and tier-3 towns with perishable products requires infrastructure investment.
3. **FSSAI Regulatory Compliance:** Obtaining licences, accurate labelling, and shelf-life certification adds cost and time for new entrants.
4. *(Optional 4th)* **Established Competition:** Dominance of established brands like Britannia and ITC limits shelf and mind share for new players.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define "Market Penetration" as a sustaining strategy for a food enterprise. Give one example.*

**Model Answer:**

> Market Penetration is a growth strategy under the Ansoff Matrix that aims to increase sales of **existing products** within **existing markets** through aggressive marketing, price incentives, and improved distribution.

**Example:** An existing Kerala *murukku* brand increases its sales in the same Kerala market by introducing combo packs, festival offers, and home-delivery tie-ups — without changing the product or the geography.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **major marketing challenges** an entrepreneur faces while introducing a new food product or service in a competitive environment. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Explain **Porter's Generic Strategies** and the **Product Life Cycle (PLC)** with reference to the Indian food industry. (7 marks)

##### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

The challenges are classified into four domains:

**1. Consumer-Side Challenges (2 marks)**
- Trial resistance due to cultural food habits.
- Sensory rejection risk (food is judged within 5 seconds of consumption).
- Health and ingredient-scrutiny from informed consumers.
- Price sensitivity in staple categories.

**2. Firm-Side / Operational Challenges (2 marks)**
- Perishability and cold-chain dependency.
- Working capital pressure from inventory holding.
- Scale-up inconsistency.
- Limited access to modern retail.

**3. Regulatory and Environmental Challenges (1.5 marks)**
- FSSAI 2006 licensing delays and costs.
- Need for AGMARK / Organic / ISI certifications.
- Sustainability expectations: biodegradable packaging, carbon disclosure.

**4. Competitive Challenges (1.5 marks)**
- Entrenched brand equity of incumbents.
- Private-label imitation by retailers.
- Near-zero switching cost for consumers.

> *Closing remark (for full marks):* A new food enterprise must therefore adopt a **bundled approach** — combining product innovation, regulatory compliance, channel strategy, and brand-building — to convert challenges into competitive advantages.

##### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Porter's Generic Strategies (3.5 marks)**

Michael Porter identified three routes to competitive advantage:

1. **Cost Leadership:** Becoming the lowest-cost producer (e.g., Haldiram's namkeen at scale). The firm earns above-average returns by selling at competitive prices with healthy margins.
2. **Differentiation:** Offering a uniquely valued product (e.g., ITC's *Aashirvaad* with consistent atta quality) that commands a price premium.
3. **Focus:** Concentrating on a narrow market segment (e.g., *Soulfull* ragi-based breakfast for diabetic consumers).

**Stuck-in-the-Middle Risk:** A firm that pursues none of these clearly is strategically vulnerable.

**Product Life Cycle — Indian Food Industry Examples (3.5 marks)**

| Stage | Description | Indian Food Example |
|-------|-------------|---------------------|
| Introduction | Low sales, high promotion cost | Cold-pressed coconut oil in metros |
| Growth | Rapid sales, new entrants | Greek yogurt in India |
| Maturity | Stable sales, intense competition | Noodles / ketchup |
| Decline | Falling sales, exit of players | Carbonated drinks vs. healthier substitutes |

**Marketing actions in each stage:**
- *Introduction:* Sampling, influencer marketing, awareness campaigns.
- *Growth:* Brand-building, distribution expansion, line extensions.
- *Maturity:* Loyalty programs, price defence, packaging innovation.
- *Decline:* Harvest strategy, niche retention, product pivot.

> *Closing remark:* Sustaining in a competitive environment requires the firm to **constantly refresh** its PLC strategy by launching extensions, entering new markets, or re-segmenting — the hallmark of long-lived food brands like *Amul* and *Britannia*.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a suitable **Ansoff Matrix**, explain the four growth strategies available to a food entrepreneur. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A small-scale food entrepreneur incurred a **fixed cost of ₹ 6,00,000** for launching an organic pickle brand. The **variable cost per kg is ₹ 150** and the **selling price is ₹ 300 per kg**. Calculate (i) break-even volume, (ii) break-even revenue, and (iii) profit if 5,000 kg are sold in a month. What strategic conclusion can the entrepreneur draw? (7 marks)

##### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

The **Ansoff Matrix** is a 2×2 framework for growth decisions based on **products (existing/new)** and **markets (existing/new)**. (1 mark for framework)

**1. Market Penetration (Existing Product + Existing Market) — 1.5 marks**
- Increase sales of current products to current customers.
- *Food example:* Pushing existing *idli dosa batter* brand into more Kerala retail outlets, with combo offers.
- Lever: pricing, advertising, loyalty programs.

**2. Market Development (Existing Product + New Market) — 1.5 marks**
- Selling the same product to a new geographic or demographic segment.
- *Food example:* A Kerala spice brand expanding to Tamil Nadu and Karnataka via e-commerce.
- Lever: distribution, regional promotion.

**3. Product Development (New Product + Existing Market) — 1.5 marks**
- Introducing a new product to current loyal customers.
- *Food example:* An existing pickle brand launching *sweetened amla preserve* for its existing buyers.
- Lever: R&D, line extension.

**4. Diversification (New Product + New Market) — 1.5 marks**
- The riskiest strategy, with both new product and new market.
- *Food example:* A Kerala-based coconut oil brand launching a carbonated coconut water in North India.

> *Closing remark:* For a *sustaining* strategy, food entrepreneurs should prioritise **Market Penetration** and **Product Development** as lower-risk options before attempting diversification.

##### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Given:** $F = 6{,}00{,}000$ ₹, $V = 150$ ₹/kg, $P = 300$ ₹/kg, $Q = 5{,}000$ kg.

**Step 1 — Contribution Margin per kg (1 mark)**

$$
P - V = 300 - 150 = 150 \,\text{₹/kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Break-Even Volume (2 marks)**

$$
Q_{BE} = \frac{F}{P - V} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{150} = 4{,}000 \,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Break-Even Revenue (2 marks)**

$$
R_{BE} = P \times Q_{BE} = 300 \times 4{,}000 = 12{,}00{,}000 \,\text{₹}
$$

**Step 4 — Profit at Q = 5,000 kg (2 marks)**

$$
\text{Profit} = (P - V) \times Q - F
$$

$$
\text{Profit} = 150 \times 5{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Profit} = 7{,}50{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}50{,}000 \,\text{₹}
$$

**Step 5 — Strategic Conclusion (extra credit)**

Since the entrepreneur breaks even at 4,000 kg and earns ₹ 1,50,000 profit at 5,000 kg, the business is **viable at current scale**. To *sustain* in a competitive environment, the entrepreneur should:
- Aim to grow sales above 6,000 kg/month to improve margins.
- Consider **Market Penetration** (deeper distribution) and **Product Development** (new pickle variants) to build defensibility.
- Maintain CAC low by leveraging local word-of-mouth and digital channels.

---

### 5.6 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Mere Listing of Challenges (0.5–1 mark penalty):** Students often write bullet points without classifying them (consumer-side vs firm-side vs regulatory). Examiners expect **classification + brief explanation** (2–3 lines per point).
>
> 2. **Confusing the 4Ps with 7Ps:** The 7Ps adds *People, Process, Physical Evidence*. Using 4Ps only is acceptable in food-retail but loses marks in service-food businesses (e.g., cloud kitchens, catering).
>
> 3. **Skipping the Numerical Working in Part B (b):** Even if the formula is correct, *not showing the substitution step* (e.g., directly writing $Q_{BE} = 4{,}000$ kg) attracts a **0.5–1 mark deduction**. Always show: *Given → Formula → Substitution → Answer → Interpretation*.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Interpretation Step:** In break-even and CAGR problems, the examiner awards the last 0.5–1 mark for **strategic interpretation**, not just numerical output. Always close with: *"This implies the entrepreneur should…"*
>
> 5. **Wrong Units in MS and CAGR:** Market Share is in **%**, CAGR is in **% per annum**, Break-even is in **units** (or kg). Mixing them up is a common, easily avoidable mistake.
>
> 6. **Failing to Map to Ansoff / Porter:** When asked about "growth" or "sustainability," students should *explicitly name* the framework (Ansoff Matrix, Porter, PLC) and link the answer to it. Generic answers lose up to 2 marks.

---

### 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Marketing Challenges and Approaches for New Products and Services Sustaining in Competitive Environment":**
>
> - **Definition:** Marketing challenges = barriers to launch; Sustainability = long-term defensive position.
> - **Four Challenge Domains:** Consumer-side, Firm/Operational, Regulatory, Competitive.
> - **Key Frameworks (must memorise names + 1-line use):** Porter's 3 Generic Strategies, Ansoff Matrix, BCG Matrix, Product Life Cycle, 4Ps/7Ps.
> - **Numerical Formulas (must be fluent):** Break-even volume, Market Share, CAGR, CAC, CRR, NPS, ROMI.
> - **Sustainability Levers:** Differentiation, brand equity, line extension, customer loyalty, regulatory compliance, sustainable packaging.
> - **Indian Food Industry Examples to Quote in Answers:** Amul (cooperative scale), ITC Aashirvaad (differentiation), Maggi (PLC revival), Haldiram's (cost leadership), BigBasket private label (competitive threat).
> - **FSSAI 2006** is the most important regulation — reference it whenever discussing regulatory challenges.
> - **Always close** a marketing/sustainability answer with a *strategic recommendation* (e.g., "the firm should adopt market penetration + product development as the dual growth lever").
> - **Common Numerical Trap:** Break-even revenue = $P \times Q_{BE}$, **not** total variable cost. Read the question carefully.
> - **High-weight keywords for examiner psychology:** "defensibility," "durable competitive advantage," "value proposition," "customer lifetime value," "category entry point," "first-mover advantage."
